title: The Incarnation Is the Model for Marriage
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Incarnation is the Model for Marriage (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Continuing the application of the incarnation to marriage
The danger of bitterness in marriage leading to jealousy and competition
Colossians 3:19: husbands, love your wives and do not be bitter toward them
The incarnation of Christ as the cure for husbandly bitterness and anger
Practical application: love as Christ loved
summary: Continuing the Advent series on the incarnation as a model for Christian living, specifically applied to marriage. Expounding Colossians 3:19, the sermon confronts the danger of husbandly bitterness and anger, which leads to jealousy and competition rather than love. Christ's humble, self-giving incarnation is the antidote—setting the pattern for how husbands are to love their wives without bitterness.
transcript:
Paul is commanding us husbands, by the grace of God, not to have a bitter attitude toward our wives. Bitterness will lead to jealousy and competition — in the family, in any relationship. Bitterness is going to produce that. In Acts 8:23, when Peter speaks to Simon the sorcerer, he says, *For I see that you are poisoned by bitterness and bound by iniquity.* No matter what your wife has done, if you are bitter toward her, you are the problem — you are poisoning the relationship. You need to repent from that bitterness.

If you want to be a Christ-like husband, bitterness is a characteristic of a man without Christ. Flip back in your Bible to Ephesians 4:31–32. Paul says, *Let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor, and evil speaking be put away from you, with all malice. And be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you.*

Paul follows a particular pattern starting in Ephesians 4:17 — he instructs us to put off the things consistent with the old man, to renew our minds, and then to put on what is consistent with one who believes in and has been renewed by the Spirit of the Lord Jesus Christ. Bitterness is something to be taken off — it is not consistent with a profession of faith in Christ. What is consistent? Kindness, tenderness, and forgiveness, instead of bitterness.

Bitterness causes us to miss out on the grace of God, and thus generates disunity in the family. In Hebrews 12:15, the Holy Spirit says that we are to look carefully *lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up cause trouble, and by this many become defiled.* And the same passage tells us that we are to pursue peace with all people, and holiness — without which no one will see the Lord. Holiness is the solution for bitterness — not a self-righteous holiness mustered up by our own efforts, but a holiness generated by the grace of God and grounded on repentance.

So the Christian husband will pursue holiness because of who he is in Christ, and will not let bitterness characterize his relationship to his wife.

## The Pattern: Put Off, Put On

In Ephesians 5, Paul tells us to put off bitterness and to put on love for our wives. It is interesting that someone could accuse Paul of merely going along with the cultural norms of the first century by telling the wife to submit to the husband. But I have never heard anyone make the argument that Paul was being culturally bound when he commanded the husband to love the wife. As a matter of fact, nothing could be more countercultural than telling a first-century Christian man that he should value and love his wife with the same value that Christ gives His church. This was a revolutionary concept in a society where wives were practically owned by their husbands. And yet Paul says: you do not own your wife — you love her, as Christ loves the church.

This is another evidence that the faithful teaching of Scripture is liberating rather than repressive.

## The Pattern of Love

As we see the pattern — the example — for a husband's love for his wife, it is Christ's love for His church. Let me ask you: how much did the church do to convince Christ to love her? Not a thing. In fact it was worse than nothing — the church was running away from Christ, cursing at Him, wanting nothing to do with Him, living in rebellion against Him — and yet He loved the church. What does that tell you as a husband? It tells you this: no matter what your wife is doing, your duty is to love her. It is a love that goes beyond unconditional. Christ's love for the church is contra-conditional — it is *despite* the condition of the church. He loves her.

The world has cheapened the meaning of love, so we need to be reminded of what the true meaning is. Remember the old Bud Light commercial with the tagline "I love you, man"? The guy was saying it to get the other guy's beer. That is the world's version of love — something we say to get something from somebody else. But that is not the love God calls us to have for our wives.

In 1 Corinthians 13 Paul says that love suffers long and is kind — the word for patience there carries the idea of bearing with someone who may be acting against you. Love does not envy. Love does not parade itself, is not puffed up. There is an eyewitness account of a radio program where a baritone singer was introduced as about to sing "How Great I Am" instead of "How Great Thou Art" — and the announcer refused to believe it until the tape was played back. But that is often our attitude as husbands: *Look how much I do for my wife. Somebody should build a statue of me.* That is not love. Love is patterned on Christ, who kept on giving even when all His people betrayed Him.

Love does not behave rudely, does not seek its own, is not provoked, thinks no evil, does not rejoice in iniquity but rejoices in the truth. It bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things. Love never fails.

Our love for our wives is not dependent on the worthiness of the beloved, because Christ loved you when you were not worthy to be loved. At your worst, Christ loved you. That is the kind of love you are called to have for your wife. Seek her good whether she deserves it or not. Christ-like love is not merely a feeling or a passion — it is a commitment.

## The Husband as Initiator

The husband is not to be passive. The husband is to be the initiator of love. In verse 25 Paul says, "Husbands, love your wives." You be the originator, the instigator, the one who starts things. Other passages imply that the wife is also to love her husband, and everything said about love applies there too — but here the command is directed explicitly to you, husband.

## Sacrificial Love

We are called to love our wives sacrificially — *just as Christ also loved the church and gave Himself for her.* But this does not mean carrying your cross with a visible show of martyrdom: *I'm just carrying my cross loving you. Look how sacrificial I am.* Christ never did that. He sacrificed Himself for His church for the joy set before Him (Hebrews 12:2). It is like fasting — the Bible says do not look like you are fasting when you are fasting. Do not put on a show when you sacrifice for your wife.

And Christ's love for the church is particular — it is not general. In John 10:11 Jesus says, *I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd gives His life for the sheep* — His sheep, specific and particular. That directed, particular love is the pattern.

So we are to give up our lives for our brides. And I do not mean jumping in front of a bullet — though you should, if it comes to that. But that is actually the easy part. The hard part is dying daily to yourself in loving your wife. Being present with her even when her mind is occupied elsewhere. Being there every day, even when there seems to be nothing given back. Brothers, Christ calls us to sacrifice our rights and our interests for the good of our wives. If you are in Christ, you have the power, the strength, and the grace to do that.

> *"For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give His life a ransom for many."* (Mark 10:45)

## A Purifying Love

Paul also says we are to love our wives with a purifying love — verses 26–27. Christ our bridegroom is committed to making us holy, and your love for your wife should make her spiritually more beautiful. Is your wife more beautiful in the sight of Christ because of you? Do you have the courage to make biblical decisions that go against her preferences when necessary — because that is what God calls you to do in holiness, for the best of both of you?

## Love Your Wife as Your Own Body

If that were not illustration enough, Paul says: love your wife as you love yourself. Verse 28: *So husbands ought to love their own wives as their own bodies.* Most people do love themselves — if you are honest, you are number one on your own list. Think about it: that is how you are to love your wife. You are united — Genesis 2:24 says you are one. If you are not functioning as one, you are broken. Your wife is not just a part of yourself — she is you. She is part of you. Loving her is loving yourself. It is unnatural to abuse your own body — that is the result of sin. So abusive behavior toward your wife is likewise the result of sin.

## Nourishing Her

Paul also says we are to love our wives by nourishing them — verse 29. And brothers, Christ has already given you everything you need for that. In 2 Peter 1:2–3, Peter says that God's divine power has given us *all things that pertain to life and godliness* — already given, not something coming in the future. He has given us all we need to nourish and love our wives. So we provide for her materially, spiritually, and in every other way.

## Cherishing Her

And we are to cherish our wives — enjoying, valuing, and treasuring them. Treasure your wife as the most precious treasure in your life. Take time to understand her. In 1 Peter 3:7, Peter says: *Husbands, likewise, dwell with them with understanding, giving honor to the wife.* If you do not do that — if you do not treasure her, understand her, and honor her — your prayers will be hindered.

Part of cherishing your wife is being patient and gentle with her. Proverbs 31:11 says, *The heart of her husband safely trusts her.* Part of cherishing is expressing appreciation and affection. Proverbs 31:28 says, *Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praises her.*

## Leading Her

And Paul says we are to love our wives by leading them — verses 23–24. The pattern of leadership in the Bible is not autocratic, not tyrannical. Jesus makes that point in Matthew 20: you do not lead the way the world does — you lead by serving. That is why the imagery of the shepherd is so important. The shepherd does not drive the sheep from behind like a cattle herder. He walks ahead of them, leads the path, and the sheep follow. That is the kind of authority we have in our families: the authority to serve.

Leading your wife in the Word means you yourself must be in the Word. It means having a plan for growth, humbly guiding your family in the Scriptures. Leading in righteousness — do not expect your wife to follow the Lord when you are neglecting to do so. Leading in selflessness, in intimacy, in decision-making — all of that is part of leading.

As the head of the wife, the husband is the provider. 1 Timothy 5:8 says that one who does not provide for his own family is worse than an unbeliever. As the head, the husband is a prophet — he brings God's Word to the family. As the husband is a leader, he is the family's priest — he brings the family before God in prayer. Brothers, if you are not praying for your wife with biblical prayers, you are not leading. And you are the protector of your wife — the Bible calls her the weaker vessel, and the husband is her protector.

But above all, brothers: love your wife by being a faithful follower of Christ. If you cannot remember anything else, remember this. This is a tall order, but it is what God calls husbands to do. It is not easy because our natural inclination is to selfishness. God will give you the grace to love your wife this way if you rely on Him for all you need.

Remember that Christ died and rose again to give you all the grace you need to follow Him — and that includes loving your wife as Christ loves you. Keep that in mind: Christ will love you with the same kind of love you show your wife. Is that what you want from Him?
